paper_title: Why Muon Outperforms Adam: A Curvature Perspective

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Muon improves training efficiency over Adam in large language-model training by about two times, but the local geometric source of this advantage remains unclear. Our work takes a first step toward demystifying Muon's superiority over Adam from a curvature perspective. First, we apply a second-order Taylor approximation to the training landscape and show that Muon achieves a larger one-step loss decrease than Adam at matched validation loss. The two optimizers have comparable first-order gains, but Muon consistently incurs a smaller second-order curvature penalty. Second, we decompose this curvature penalty into the squared update norm and Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS). We find that Muon and Adam have comparable update norms, so Muon's smaller curvature penalty is driven by lower NDS, not update scale. Third, we study how training data and model structure shape Muon's NDS advantage. Using Zipf-Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) data with controlled imbalance, we show that data imbalance amplifies Muon's NDS advantage over Adam. A within-/cross-layer decomposition further shows that, in the middle and late stages of training, Muon's lower NDS is mainly sustained by smaller within-layer curvature. Beyond empirical evidence, we analyze stylized quadratic problems with heterogeneous curvature and gradient alignment toward high-curvature modes. We prove that Muon attains a smaller average NDS than GD by balancing update energy across curvature groups; when curvature heterogeneity is sufficiently strong, this also yields lower local quadratic loss after the same number of steps.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Muon has emerged as powerful alternative to Adam for Large Language Model (LLM) pretraining (Jordan et al., 2024b). It exploits the matrix structure of parameters by spectrally normalizing the gradient momentum matrix, effectively setting its nonzero singular values to the same scale. This matrix-aware design enables Muon to achieve up to about 2 faster training than Adam in LLM pretraining across various model scales (Liu et al., 2025b; Jordan et al., 2024a; Shah et al., 2025). Recent works have sought to explain Muons advantage over Adam through the lenses of associative memory and data long-tailedness (Wang et al., 2025b; Vasudeva et al., 2025b). Different from existing mechanistic interpretations of Muons superiority, we take first step toward understanding Muons advantage over Adam from the perspective of the optimization landscape. Specifically, we ask the following questions: Equal contribution. Project Lead. Email: Corresponding to fzzhang@u.nus.edu, zhuoran.yang@yale.edu 6 2 0 2 ] . [ 1 2 6 6 4 0 . 6 0 6 2 : r 1 1. What landscape property supports Muons advantage over Adam? 2. How do pretraining factors, such as the training data and model structure, influence this property? To answer the first question, we analyze the one-step loss decrease of Muon and Adam via second-order Taylor expansion of the training loss. We find that this expansion accurately predicts the realized loss decrease and reveals that, while the two optimizers achieve comparable first-order (gradient alignment) gains, Muon incurs much smaller curvature penalty. That is, Muons larger realized loss decrease is primarily driven by its smaller second-order curvature cost. We further decompose the curvature penalty into contributions from the update norm and Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS), showing that Muons smaller curvature penalty originates from lower NDS, which is determined by the update direction, rather than from smaller update norms. Thus, our analysis identifies lower NDS as the main contributor to Muons superiority over Adam. To answer the second question, we investigate how training data and model structure contribute to Muons smaller NDS. Motivated by Wang et al. (2025b); Vasudeva et al. (2025b), we first examine the role of data imbalance by training on synthetic data generated from Zipf-Probabilistic Context-Free Grammar (PCFG) with controlled imbalance levels. We find that Muons NDS advantage over Adam becomes larger as the dataset becomes more imbalanced. Complementing the data perspective, we then turn to the model-structure perspective and decompose NDS into within-layer and cross-layer contributions. This decomposition shows that Muons cross-layer contribution decreases rapidly during training, so in the middle and late stages of pretraining, its smaller NDS is mainly sustained by smaller within-layer NDS. To theoretically understand these observations, we study Muons behavior on stylized quadratic optimization problems designed to mirror the landscape characteristics of LLM training. On these problems, Adam and GD empirically exhibit comparable NDS and loss decreases, so we focus the analysis on GD and Muon for simplicity. Under heterogeneous curvatures and gradient alignment with high-curvature directions, we prove that Muon has smaller average NDS than GD because its update balances highand low-curvature directions more evenly. Moreover, when curvature heterogeneity is sufficiently strong, Muon achieves smaller loss than GD after the same number of steps. These results provide theoretical support for our main empirical findings. Summarizing the empirical and theoretical findings, we identify concrete mechanism behind Muons advantage over Adam. Muon achieves larger one-step loss decrease than Adam because its spectrally normalized update direction has lower NDS, thereby incurring smaller second-order curvature cost.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Muon and structured matrix updates. Recent works study Muon from empirical, algorithmic, and geometric perspectives. Jordan et al. (2024b) first established Muons gains on matrix-valued parameters, while Shah et al. (2025); Liu et al. (2025b); Sato et al. (2025); Li and Hong (2025); Kim and Oh (2026); Si et al. (2025) examine its practical behavior, scalability, convergence properties, and algorithmic variants. One line of follow-up work asks what geometry Muons orthogonalized update is implicitly optimizing, as reflected by Chen et al. (2025); Ma et al. (2026); Shulgin et al. (2025); Kim and Oh (2026). Another line studies how to make orthogonalized matrix updates cheaper, more scalable, or more robust in practical training systems. This includes distributed, periodic, low-rank, adaptive, federated, quantized, and long-tailed variants such as Ahn et al. (2025); Khaled et al. (2025); Si et al. (2025); He et al. (2025); Li et al. (2025); Liu et al. (2025a); Zhang et al. (2026a,b); Liu et al. (2026); Cheng et al. (2026). Because Muons practical implementation depends on approximate polar or NewtonSchulz maps, work on faster orthogonalization, including Grishina et al. (2025); Hu et al. (2026); Boissin et al. (2025), is also closely connected. At broader level, Bernstein and Newhouse (2024b); Kovalev (2025); Pethick et al. (2025); Lau et al. (2025); An et al. (2025); Wen et al. 2 (2025) place Muon and related orthogonalized or structured updates inside larger geometric family. This broader structured-update viewpoint also connects Muon to Bernstein and Newhouse (2024a); Pethick et al. (2025); Lau et al. (2025); An et al. (2025), and matrixor block-aware preconditioning methods such as Vyas et al. (2024); Shazeer and Stern (2018); Anil et al. (2020); Martens and Grosse (2015); Gupta et al. (2018). There is also line of work understanding and explaining why Muon outperforms Adam as Wang et al. (2025b); Li et al. (2026); Kim et al. (2026); Vasudeva et al. (2025a); Qi et al. (2026). Our results are also complementary to the memory-centric explanation of Wang et al. (2025b), which attributes Muons advantage to more balanced tail-end associative memory learning under heavy-tailed distributions. However, we identify local curvature mechanism: at the same iterate, Muon can pay smaller directional sharpness penalty than Adam along its matrix update. Adam and adaptive optimization. Our comparison also connects to the large literature on Adam and adaptive gradient methods, beginning with (Kingma and Ba, 2014; Duchi et al., 2011). This literature ranges from decoupled regularization and convergence theory, as in Loshchilov and Hutter (2019); Reddi et al. (2019); Chen et al. (2019); Zhou et al. (2018); Defossez et al. (2020); Guo et al. (2021); Li et al. (2023); Zhang et al. (2022); Zou et al. (2019); Liu et al. (2019); Zhuang et al. (2020); Zhang et al. (2024b). Other works examine why adaptive methods behave differently from SGD or spectral-gradient methods in language modeling, imbalanced data, and Transformer training (Kunstner et al., 2024; Vasudeva et al., 2025a; Pan and Li, 2023; Zhang et al., 2024a). We do not analyze Adams convergence in this work. We use it as the baseline and compare the curvature it encounters along its matrix update against the corresponding Muon update. Sharpness, curvature, and optimization geometry. large body of literature studies the role of sharpness and curvature in deep learning optimization and generalization. Early work connected large-batch training, minima geometry, and generalization, while also pointing out that naive sharpness notions can be sensitive to parameterization. This line includes Keskar et al. (2016); Dinh et al. (2017); Li et al. (2018); Izmailov et al. (2018). Another line studies sharpness as training-dynamics or algorithm-design quantity, including sharpness-aware minimization and its efficient variants (Cohen et al., 2021; Foret et al., 2020; Kwon et al., 2021; Andriushchenko et al., 2023; Wen et al., 2023; Du et al., 2021, 2022). Closer to our setting, recent Transformer and LLM studies connect curvature heterogeneity to optimization behavior, including Zhang et al. (2024a); Wang et al. (2025a); Pan and Li (2023); Kalra et al. (2026). These works primarily analyze global sharpness, flatness, or curvature-aware training rules. We adopt local, optimizerdependent viewpoint: because Muon and Adam induce different update matrices at the same parameter point, we compare the Hessian curvature along each update direction, which explains why the optimizer gap appears in the second-order term even when the first-order gains are similar.

sections.title: 4 Main Results
sections.content: In this section, we present the main empirical findings that characterize Muons advantage over Adam from curvature perspective. In Section 4.1, we show that Muon achieves larger one-step loss decrease than Adam, and that this advantage is driven by smaller second-order curvature penalty rather than larger first-order gain. In Section 4.2, we decompose the curvature penalty and identify lower NDS as the source of Muons smaller curvature cost. In Section 4.3, we examine how dataset imbalance amplifies the NDS gap between the two optimizers. Finally, in Section 4.4, we decompose NDS into within-layer and cross-layer contributions to understand how different layers contribute to Muons curvature advantage.

sections.title: 4.2 Muon’s Smaller Curvature Penalty Comes from Its Update Direction
sections.content: Observation 1 shows that Muons advantage in one-step loss decrease is primarily explained by its smaller second-order curvature cost. Note that the curvature penalty (2) (W, Z) = 1/2 Z, H[Z] depends on both the scale of the update and the curvature along its direction. We now ask: does Muons smaller curvature cost arise because it takes smaller steps, or because its update direction encounters less curvature? To disentangle the effects of update direction and update scale, we measure the curvature of the local quadratic form by normalizing out the scale. Specifically, following Pan and Li (2023), we define the Normalized Directional Sharpness (NDS) along nonzero update as SF (W ; Z) = Z, H[Z]/Z2 . (4.2) With this definition, the curvature penalty factorizes as (2) SF (W ; Z), so smaller curvature cost can only come from smaller update norm or lower NDS. We therefore examine whether Muons smaller curvature penalty is driven by smaller update norm or by smaller NDS. Observations 1 and 2 show that Muons larger one-step loss decrease is driven by smaller second-order curvature penalty, which in turn arises from lower NDS rather than smaller update norms. We next investigate how this curvature advantage depends on two key factors: the training data and the model architecture. On the data side, we identify which data properties affect the magnitude of Muons curvature advantage. On the model side, we examine how the interaction between different layers contributes to this advantage.

sections.title: 4.3 Dataset Imbalance Widens the NDS Gap between Adam and Muon
sections.content: Prior work suggests that the tail structure and imbalance of training data can interact strongly with optimizer behavior in two ways. First, Hessian analyses of neural networks show that the Hessian spectrum depends sensitively on the data mixture (Sagun et al., 2017; Papyan, 2018). Second, recent studies find that Muon can outperform Adam especially on heavy-tailed data (Wang et al., 2025b; Vasudeva et al., 2025b). Motivated by these findings, we study whether dataset imbalance amplifies the normalized directional-sharpness gap identified above. Experiment setup: data generation. To study how data imbalance affects NDS, we construct synthetic training data using Zipf-PCFG, which allows us to explicitly control the degree of imbalance. Specifically, we instantiate Zipf-PCFG with topics [K] and partition the vocabulary into token classes V1, . . . , VC, so that = c=1Vc. Different token classes correspond to different sentence components, such as nouns, adjectives, and verbs. Each topic has its own preference distribution within each token class c. For example, the topic = food assigns higher probabilities to eating-related verbs. Given topic and token class c, let ϕk,c(j) denote the base probability of token Vc. To introduce controllable imbalance, we rank tokens in Vc in decreasing order of ϕk,c(j) and denote the rank of token by r(j, c, k), where the most probable token has rank 1 and r(j, c, k) {1, . . . , Vc}. At imbalance level s, we sample tokens from the reweighted distribution proportional to r(j, c, k)sϕk,c(j). We then train 9M-parameter NanoGPT model (4 layers, 4 attention heads, model dimension 256) on datasets generated under imbalance levels {0, 0.5, 1} for 10,000 steps, using both Adam and Muon with learning rates selected by grid search. Additional experimental details are provided in Appendix A. Metric: trajectory-averaged NDS. To evaluate NDS along the full training trajectory, for each optimizer 7 opt {Muon, Adam}, we define the trajectory-averaged NDS at imbalance level as Sopt(s) = (cid:88) tT SF (W opt,s ; opt,s )/T , where denotes the set of training steps, {0, 0.5, 1} is the imbalance level, and opt,s denote the parameter and update induced by optimizer opt under imbalance level at step t. To highlight the difference between Adam and Muon, we normalize Sopt(s) by Muons value at = 0: (cid:101)Sopt(s) = Sopt(s)/ SMuon(0). We further define the normalized sharpness gap at imbalance level as (s) = (cid:101)SAdam(s) (cid:101)SMuon(s). and opt,s Experiment findings. In Figure 3(a) we plot the normalized trajectory-averaged NDS (cid:101)Sopt(s) against the Zipf exponent {0, 0.5, 1}. As shown in this figure, both optimizers NDS increases monotonically with imbalance, but the effect is far stronger for Adam: Adams normalized NDS rises from 1.63 to 2.38 as increases from 0 to 1, whereas Muons grows only from 1.00 to 1.25. The widening shaded region between the two curves reflects the growing gap. sections.title: 5 A Case Study of Structured Matrix-Block Quadratic Models
sections.content: Section 4 establishes that Muon achieves larger one-step loss decrease than Adam due to lower NDS (Observations 12), that data imbalance amplifies this gap (Observation 3), and that Muons NDS advantage increasingly concentrates in within-layer Hessian blocks as training proceeds (Observation 4). In this section, we provide theoretical justification for these empirical findings. Since Observation 4 shows that within-layer curvature becomes the dominant component of Muons NDS advantage, we isolate single weight matrix and study the local curvature within this block on quadratic model, comparing the curvature encountered by update directions induced by different optimizers.

sections.title: 5.1 Structured Quadratic Model
sections.content: Motivated by the second-order Taylor approximation in Eqn. (4.1), we focus on the local quadratic landscape around one optimization step in LLM pretraining. Each assumption is empirically verified in real pretraining dynamics: we provide in-text verification figures below and additional details in Appendix D. We begin with decomposition of the Hessian. Let α = m/q denote the relative size of the high-curvature group, and let ρ = wH/wL > 1 denote the curvature ratio. Under Assumptions 5.15.4, the following result holds. Smaller NDS of Muon. For every finite horizon 1, Muon has smaller finite-horizon averaged NDS than that of GD, i.e., Muon < GD . Larger Loss Decrease of Muon. If ρ + 1 > 1/α > 1 + σH/σL , for every finite horizon 1, Muon ) < Q(Y GD achieves lower loss than GD, i.e., Q(Y Muon ). Theorem 5.5 gives two results. First, it shows that Muon has smaller average NDS than that of GD for any finite horizon, providing theoretical support for Observation 2. Second, under sufficient curvature heterogeneityspecifically, when the curvature ratio satisfies ρ > 1/α 1 and the high-curvature group is small enough that α < σL/(σL + σH)Muon achieves larger cumulative loss decrease than GD, supporting Observation 1. The condition ρ > 1/α 1 requires the largest curvature values to be much larger than the others, consistent with Hessian eigenvalue outliers observed in prior work (Sagun et al., 2017; Fort and Ganguli, 2019). The condition α < 1/(1 + σH/σL) requires the high-curvature group to occupy sufficiently small fraction of the positive-curvature subspace, consistent with the small proportion of outlier curvature directions observed in practice (Sagun et al., 2017). The key mechanism behind both results is that Muons spectral normalization equalizes the update amplitude across all orthogonal curvature eigenmodes, distributing energy evenly between highand lowcurvature directions. In contrast, GDs update is proportional to the gradient, which concentrates more energy on high-curvature directions (by Assumption 5.4). This concentration causes GD to incur larger directional sharpness and, when curvature heterogeneity is strong enough, larger curvature penalty that offsets its first-order gain. We also highlight that our curvature perspective helps explain why some Muon variants improve training efficiency. The recent work of Zhu et al. (2026) enhances Muon by increasing its update component toward flat Hessian directions, leading to further efficiency gains. This finding is supported by our analysis: shifting the update toward flatter directions should reduce NDS, thereby lowering the curvature penalty and potentially increasing the one-step loss decrease.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: Our work takes first step toward understanding Muons superiority over Adam from curvature perspective. We show that Muons lower NDS reduces its curvature penalty, thereby yielding larger one-step loss decrease than Adam. We further show that this NDS advantage is shaped by data imbalance and layer-wise interactions in the model. We also provide quadratic-model analysis that theoretically establishes Muons advantages in NDS and one-step loss decrease. limitation of our work is that we focus on causal LLMs; we leave the verification of these insights on other model classes, such as diffusion models, to future work.

sections.title: A Experimental Details
sections.content: A.1 FineWeb Main-Text Experiments We use 124M-parameter NanoGPT model with 12 Transformer layers, 12 attention heads, and hidden dimension of 768. The vocabulary size is 50,257 under the GPT-2 tokenizer. Training is conducted on FineWeb-10B with sequence length 1024 (Penedo et al., 2024). For Muon, we apply Muon to all matrix parameters except the token embeddings and language-model head, using momentum µ = 0.95 and no weight decay. The momentum coefficient is linearly warmed up from 0.85 to 0.95 over the first 300 steps, and NewtonSchulz orthogonalization uses 5 iterations. The embedding layer, lm head, and all scalar or one-dimensional parameters are optimized by Adam with β1 = 0.8 and β2 = 0.95. For the Adam baseline, all parameters, including the attention and MLP matrices, are optimized by Adam with β1 = 0.8 and β2 = 0.95. For both Adam and Muon, learning rates are selected by grid search from {1, 2, 5} {101, 102, 103, 104}. We use Modded-NanoGPT as our code base (Jordan et al., 2024a). Directional sharpness is computed every 500 steps through Hessianvector products. We conduct experiments on 4 A100 with 80 GB memory. A.2 Zipf-PCFG Dataset Construction and Experiment details in Section 4.3 A.2.1 Dataset Construction The dataset used in Section 4.3 is synthetic Zipf-PCFG corpus with latent topic structure and explicit grammatical constraints. Vocabulary. The vocabulary contains 4,411 tokens partitioned into = 20 grammatical classes (the 4,412nd slot is the EOS token) so that = c=1Vc. Class sizes are fixed at Nanim = 700, Ninanim = 1200, Vintrans = 380, Vtrans = 570, Vclause = 280, Vdative = 190, Vmodal = 10, Vneg = 5, Adjgrad = 380, Adjnongrad = 330, Advdeg = 20, Advmanner = 280, = 25, Detdef = Detindef = Comp = Coord = Punc = 5, Numsg = Numpl = 8. Each class has its own internal Zipf rank. Latent topics. We instantiate = 30 latent topics. For each topic and each class we draw per-topic affinity ϕk,c c1 by normalising Gamma(αϕ, 1) with αϕ = 0.3, so that affinities are sparse over class tokens. The topic prior is π Dirichlet(απ = 1.0). Documents drift through topics under Markov chain with transition matrix Tkk = 0.85, Tkℓ = 0.15/(K 1) for ℓ = k, providing strong topic persistence within each document. Token emission. Within token class and topic k, each token Vc has base topic-specific probability ϕk,c(j). We rank the tokens in Vc in decreasing order of ϕk,c(j) and denote the rank of token by r(j, c, k). Given imbalance level s, tokens are sampled from the reweighted distribution Ps(j c, k) = (cid:80) r(j, c, k)sϕk,c(j) r(j, c, k)sϕk,c(j) jVc , Vc. Thus, when = 0, token emission is governed only by the topic-specific base distribution ϕk,c; increasing further concentrates probability mass on higher-ranked tokens and yields heavier-tailed class-conditional token distributions. 21 Grammatical structure. We sample sentences from PCFG with start symbol S. Following standard linguistic notation, denotes noun phrase, verb phrase, CP complementiser phrase (clausal complement), CPfronted fronted clausal complement, RelAdv relative-adverb clause, and Coord coordinator. The seven productions are P (probability 0.55), P CP (0.12), Coord (0.08), CPfronted P (0.08), Modal-VP (0.07), Neg-VP (0.05), and P RelAdv (0.05). Each is generated under one of four verb subcategorisation frames (intransitive, transitive, clausal, dative) and respects: (i) explicit number agreement between subject and verb (with each noun assigned fixed singular/- plural feature, 40% plural); (ii) selectional restrictions, with animate nouns preferred as agents (preference 5) and inanimate nouns preferred as patients (preference 3); (iii) role coherence (the agent and patient of clause are required to differ); and (iv) negation and modality scope tracking. Recursion is bounded at depth 14. Document and tokenisation. Each sample is multi-sentence document obtained by repeatedly sampling sentences while drifting through topics under the HMM above. Documents are flattened to single token stream, mapped to the integer vocabulary, and concatenated into binary training shards. The training and validation Zipf exponents can be set independently. The main comparison fixes the validation distribution at = 0, where the rank-based Zipf bias is removed, and token emission is governed only by the topic-specific base distribution ϕk,c. Then, we vary the training exponent {0, 0.5, 1}, isolating the effect of training imbalance on optimizer behaviour. A.2.2 Experiment Setup Different from our FineWeb experiments, considering the reduced task difficulty, we use 9M-parameter NanoGPT model with 4 Transformer layers, 4 attention heads, and model dimension 256. The vocabulary size is 4,412 under the synthetic ZipfPCFG tokenizer. Training uses the ZipfPCFG corpus with sequence length 1,024 and gradient accumulation 8, giving an effective batch size of 8,192 tokens per step. Validation uses 2,457,600 tokens with sequence length 1,024. Total training is 10,000 optimization steps. We compare two optimizers. For Muon, we apply Muon to all attention matrices (WQ, WK, WV , WO) and all MLP matrices (Win, Wout), with learning rate 5 103, momentum µ = 0.95, and NewtonSchulz orthogonalization 5 iterations. The embedding, lm head, and all scalar (1D) parameters are optimized by Adam with learning rate 1 102, β1 = 0.8, β2 = 0.95. For Adam, all parameters, including the attention and MLP matrices, are optimized by Adam with learning rate 1 102, β1 = 0.8, β2 = 0.95. Additional Experimental Results to Section 4.2 This section complements the main NDS comparison results in Section 4.2 by showing the sharpness comparison over the training steps and the corresponding ratio of curvature penalty, NDS and update norm over the training steps. This result follows the same conclusion as Observation 2. Figure 8(a) shows that Muon maintains consistently smaller NDS than Adam throughout the entire training trajectory, with the gap persisting from early to late training. Figure 8(b) reports the Adam-to-Muon ratios of the curvature penalty, NDS, and squared update norm over training steps. The update norm ratio remains close to 1, confirming that the two optimizers have comparable update norms at every step. In contrast, the NDS ratio stays well above 1, with mean value of 2.94 over the trajectory. The curvaturepenalty ratio closely tracks the NDS ratio, reinforcing Observation 2 that Muons smaller curvature penalty is driven by lower NDS rather than smaller update norms. We note that the mean NDS ratio at matched training steps (2.94) is larger than the ratio at matched validation loss reported in Section 4.2 (1.76). This is because Muon reaches lower validation loss than Adam at the same training step, aligning by step effectively compares Muon at more advanced stage of optimization against Adam at less advanced stage, which amplifies the apparent gap. The matched-validation-loss (a) Sharpness comparison. (b) Adam/Muon ratios of curvature penalty, NDS, update scale Figure 8: NDS and the corresponding ratio comparison along the training steps of Muon and Adam. Panel (a): sharpness comparison. Panel (b): Adam/Muon ratios of the curvature penalty, the NDS, and the squared Frobenius update norm. comparison in the main text provides an assessment of the per-step curvature difference, while the step-aligned comparison here confirms that the qualitative conclusion of Observation 2 is robust to the choice of alignment.
